name: Flip Phillips
description: American jazz tenor saxophone and clarinet player (1915–2001)
abstract: Joseph Edward Filippelli (March 26, 1915 – August 17, 2001), known professionally as Flip Phillips, was an American jazz tenor saxophonist and clarinetist. He is best remembered for his work with Norman Granz's Jazz at the Philharmonic concerts from 1946 to 1957.

interviews.title: Flip Phillips interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 11, 1996

quotes.text: Yeah that was a good one. That came off, we had a concert at, not the Island, the Mosque Theater in Newark at eight o’clock, and at twelve o’clock we went to Carnegie Hall in New York, and that’s when it was made, in 1947. We played it and everybody was relaxed and it just came off. It was wonderful you know at the time, and we didn’t think anything of it. And then when the record — all the disc jockeys started playing it. And it was quite a thing. It sold a million albums. For an instrumental, that’s great. And it was just great. We had a good rhythm section there. Jo Jones, Hank Jones, Ray Brown.
quotes.caption: Flip Phillips recalls a 1947 Carnegie Hall recording session
quotes.doc_title: Flip Phillips interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 11, 1996

quotes.text: I put it on my horn, on my saxophone case, I-W-S. Well he told you to say that. Well Benny used to pick on everybody you know. He picked on everybody but me, let’s put it that way. And I-W-S was “I Won’t Snap.” I don’t care what you say, I won’t snap you know. And when people used to get mad at him, I’d go, “I-W-S.” He’d go, “Okay.” That’s where that came from. I won’t snap. Which I did one time. No I didn’t snap, but Benny used to pick on everybody you know. So I got fed up. I went in his dressing room one time and I said, “Look, Pops, what are you putting down?” I said, “You’re the best clarinet player in the world. You played Carnegie Hall, you played Symphony Hall, you played all over, the only place you haven’t played is the moon.” And his answer was — And I said, “Go on and play your clarinet. You play better than anybody.” And his answer was, “You know, Pops, you’re right.” And since that time he went out and played and he played better than he ever played ever in his life. His wife, John Hammond came over, and said, “I never heard him play like that.” He was like a baby, he needed a little —
quotes.caption: Anecdote about Benny Goodman's Carnegie Hall performance
quotes.doc_title: Flip Phillips interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 11, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Jerry Dodgion
about_quotes.evidence: He hired Red Norvo’s quintet... and added Jack Sheldon and Bill Harris and Flip Phillips... And we toured Europe and had a great time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jerry Dodgion interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, March 9, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: John LaPorta
about_quotes.evidence: the band that I was on, 1944 to ‘46 with Dave Tough and Flip Phillips and Bill Harris and people like that.
about_quotes.doc_title: John LaPorta interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 13, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Candoli
about_quotes.evidence: The big names in the band, my brother and Bill Harris, Flip Phillips, Davey Tough, Chubby Jackson, they were all making two hundred, two fifty a week... And I was making ninety bucks a week.

about_quotes.speaker: Billy Bauer
about_quotes.evidence: I met Flip, well I guess at his birthday, but he told me that the only solo that Davy every took is on my tune, which was I think “Skyscraper” that I recorded with Flip Phillips. A big hit.
about_quotes.doc_title: Billy Bauer interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, March 22, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Howard Alden
about_quotes.evidence: I was on there playing basically with Flip Phillips and Kenny Davern... Last time I did one of those jazz cruises a couple of years ago, again, like I’d be playing with Flip Phillips
about_quotes.doc_title: Howard Alden interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Earl Watkins
about_quotes.evidence: We backed Flip Phillips, Flip Phillips came in as a single and so Vernon build a band around him... I was on drums, Flip, Jerome and Alan.
about_quotes.doc_title: Earl Watkins interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Francisco, California, August 7, 2002
